body_txt: A character is introduced as an insufferable jerk, arrogant because of some amazing talent or high station in life. Everyone hopes, as soon as they're introduced, that they're going to eventually get what's coming to them... ...and then they do. Immediately. Right at the start of the story. The character is overthrown and knocked out of that high station ; or has their marvelous talents somehow taken from them, and spends the rest of the story learning to cope, with varying levels of success. That's right, this trope begins with their fall from a position of power or influence to learn An Aesop , rather than giving them their due late in the story. Alternatively, we may meet the character just after their fall from power, and learn about their stuck-up, careless past and subsequent karmic punishment via Flash Back . The story spends just enough time showing the audience (or, if the writers are in a hurry, telling us ) that they're an arrogant bastard to convince us, before knocking them off their high horse. The major conflict of the story usually involves the character learning to be humble, or at the very least learning not to indignantly ask everyone they run into "Do you KNOW who I AM?!", as they are often, despite being hit over the head with the humility stick, still very interested in continuing their thoughtless ways. The story arc completes with the character accepting the Aesop. The Aesop can vary, but a typical lesson would be that they can't get by alone — they need other people, and other people need to be able to depend on them in return. With this change of character, the arc may continue to their return to power as a juster, kinder soul. On a cultural or setting level see Look on My Works, Ye Mighty, and Despair , Soiled City on a Hill , and And Man Grew Proud . Super-Trope to Arrogance Breeds Laziness . May overlap with Fallen Princess , Holding-Back Backfire , and Tragic Hero . Compare with The Atoner (who is necessarily repentant by definition), Break the Haughty (for a slower descent), A Taste of Power (for when this happens in video games), and Third Act Stupidity (when near the end of a story). If you're looking for pride before a literal fall, see Disney Villain Death .
